behaviors.description: Conversation history is trimmed to the most recent 10 messages when it exceeds the LLM's context window.
behaviors.justification: The real failure is silent and sneaky: we trim the history but the 10-message trim still exceeds the real LLM's token limit, or the trimming logic itself is wrong. A Unit test mocking the LLM won't reveal this—a mock accepts anything. We need either a realistic mock that enforces token constraints (Integration) or a real LLM call (System). Integration is minimum because we can mock the LLM to reject oversized inputs, validating the trim logic against token constraints without hitting the real API.
behaviors.test_description: Verify that when conversation history exceeds the context window, the chatbot trims to exactly the most recent 10 messages and that this trimmed set remains within the LLM's token limit. Test both the selection logic (message count) and the token count of the output using a mock LLM that enforces token constraints.

behaviors.description: The generated response addresses the customer's question rather than producing a generic or off-topic reply.
behaviors.justification: This is fundamentally about LLM output correctness—whether the model actually produces a relevant response. No mock can verify this; mocking would just return whatever you program it to return. The real failure is the LLM going off-topic or reverting to boilerplate. Only a real LLM call reveals whether the prompt and context actually drive relevant, customer-specific answers. This is a core Agentic failure mode: the system's behavior depends on the LLM's actual generative output.
behaviors.test_description: Verify that for multiple customer questions (e.g., password reset, billing inquiry, technical issue), the LLM generates contextually relevant responses that address the specific question, not generic fallback text. Test with varied question types and verify response relevance against explicit criteria.

behaviors.description: The tone re-check correctly identifies when a response is too formal for a casual-tier customer and triggers regeneration.
behaviors.justification: This behavior has two parts: (1) the tone-checking LLM identifies formal language, and (2) the business logic triggers regeneration. Part (2) could be tested at Unit with a mock tone-checker, but part (1)—the real failure—depends on the tone-checker LLM actually working. A mock tone checker that always returns 'too formal' or 'fine' won't catch the real failure: the tone-detection LLM misses formal language in production. Minimum level is Agentic because tone detection requires real LLM calls.
behaviors.test_description: Verify that responses written in formal register (e.g., 'I regret to inform you...') are correctly flagged by the tone re-check when the customer is casual-tier, and that regeneration is triggered with a directive to use casual language. Test with multiple tone examples (formal, casual, neutral) and confirm that formal responses trigger regeneration and casual responses do not.

behaviors.description: The full exchange (user message, generated response, any regenerations) is persisted to the database with correct timestamps.
behaviors.justification: This is a data persistence failure mode, not an LLM correctness problem. Real failures include: timestamps recorded out of order, regenerations not persisted, wrong conversation linked to wrong user, or timestamps in wrong timezone. These are caught by testing against a real or mock database. Unit tests (no I/O) cannot verify database writes. Integration testing with a mock/in-process database (SQLite, in-memory) is sufficient; System testing with a real database adds confidence but is slower and not strictly necessary to catch the logic error.
behaviors.test_description: Verify that for a conversation with one initial response and one regeneration, all three exchanges (user message, first response, regenerated response) are stored in the database with accurate, monotonically increasing timestamps. Confirm that the regeneration record correctly links to the original conversation and that the second response timestamp is later than the first.

behaviors.description: When the tone check triggers regeneration, the second response replaces the first—the customer never sees the rejected draft.
behaviors.justification: The real failure is that the customer sees the rejected (too-formal) response because either (a) the tone-checking LLM didn't flag it as problematic, or (b) the UI logic failed to hide it. Part (b) could be tested at Unit/Integration with a mock tone-checker, but part (a)—the critical failure—requires the tone-checker LLM to actually work. A mock that always flags bad responses wouldn't catch a real tone-detection miss. Minimum level is Agentic because the failure depends on the real LLM behavior.
behaviors.test_description: Verify that when a formal response is generated and flagged by the tone re-check, the system triggers regeneration, receives a revised response with casual tone, and presents only the second response to the customer. Confirm that the customer's conversation history and UI show only the final response, and the rejected first response is never visible. Test across multiple tone violation scenarios.
